text: Welcome to this video presentation about harmonic HD power right upper lobectomy performed by Mr. Felice Granato. This video demonstrated the adoption of the harmonic HD for a lobectomy in a seventy-one year old woman, smoker, but fit for surgery and with biopsy proved adenocarcinoma of the right upper lobe and staged by the CT PET as a T-one-a-n-zero-m-zero.
So she was offered an anterior approach three-posed BATS right upper lobectomy
The video shows the placement of a rigid port for the camera and a posterior port, likely used for instrument access during the pulmonary lobectomy procedure.
The surgeon retracts the lung posteriorly and begins dissection at the reflection of the mediastinal pleura onto the hilum, revealing the superior pulmonary vein.
and the apical trunk.
The surgeon uses both blunt and sharp dissection techniques with a harmonic device to divide the lymph nodes.
Station eleven.
The ultrasonic surgical device is shown with its black blade vibrating against the white blade, utilizing ultrasonic energy for tissue dissection.
The ultrasonic device generates thermic energy, simultaneously dissecting and coagulating tissue around the superior pulmonary vein as the lung is retracted posteriorly.
The surgeon carefully maneuvers the black blade of the ultrasonic device under direct vision to avoid accidental damage to surrounding anatomical structures during the dissection.
The superior pulmonary veins are linked and dissected away using an Ethicon vascular reload device, with visible separation from surrounding tissue.
With the superior pulmonary vein removed, the underlying pulmonary artery is clearly visible, revealing its anatomical structure.

The apical trunk is visible in the middle of the screen, with the superior pulmonary vein stump on the left. The apical trunk is dissected free and slinged, followed by the dissection of underlying lymph nodes. The apical trunk is then divided using an Ethicon stapler with a vascular reload.
The surgeon applies gentle retraction to position the stapler, dividing the targeted structure before proceeding to the bronchus.

The surgeon dissects a lymph node at the bifurcation of the secondary carinae onto the bronchus intermedius and right upper bronchus using a harmonic scalpel, identifying it as a landmark in the procedure.
The surgeon dissects around the right upper lobe bronchus, preparing to divide it using a green reload stapler.
The surgeon uses the harmonic device as a blunt instrument to manipulate the bronchus, creating a free wall to facilitate the passage of the stapler.
The surgeon identifies the small recurrent artery in the surgical field, preparing it for further dissection or division.

The surgeon uses an energy device, such as the harmonic, to seal and divide small vessels up to seven millimeters in diameter, providing a clear view of the pulmonary artery anatomy.

The surgeon confirms that no additional branches are supplying the upper lobe, with the AE-VI artery providing blood supply to the apical segment of the lower lobe. Further posterior mediastinal dissection is performed before completing the procedure with the final stapler firing.
The surgeon uses the harmonic device to achieve aerostatic results while manipulating the bronchus, providing a clear view of the surrounding anatomy.
The surgeon places the resected lung specimen into a retrieval bag for removal from the chest cavity.
The surgeon proceeds with the lymphadenectomy at station IV-R, carefully dissecting and removing lymphatic tissue from the right side of the mediastinum.
The surgeon opens the pleura over the azygos vein, exposing the underlying anatomical structures for further dissection.
The surgeon uses the harmonic device to seal small lymphatic vessels, achieving effective hemostasis during the pulmonary lobectomy procedure.

The surgeon uses both blunt and sharp dissection techniques, ensuring the black blade remains away from vital structures such as the superior vena cava (SVC) and the azygos vein to prevent accidental bleeding.

The lymph node grasper retracts the specimen to the left, while the harmonic device performs dissection on the right side of the screen. The space is gradually cleared, revealing the trachea posteriorly and the azygos vein.
The surgeon closes the space at the bottom of the screen, with the superior vena cava (SVC) visible anteriorly.
The surgeon identifies the arch of the aorta as the deep medial structure within the anatomical space being dissected.
A nice hand block dissection of station four R and two R is recommended and also possible either by open surgery or by VAS surgery as in this case.
The surgeon retracts the lung upwards to expose and divide the pulmonary ligament, preparing to harvest the specimen.

The surgeon retracts the lung anteriorly while completing the posterior mediastinal dissection upwards. The station nine lymph node and ligament are retracted superiorly, and a station ten lymph node is harvested over the inferior pulmonary vein. The dissection progresses over the superior margin of the inferior pulmonary vein and just below the carina.
The surgeon dissects the subcarinal region, focusing on the removal of station seven lymph nodes, with visible manipulation of surrounding tissues.
The esophagus is visibly retracted using a suction device, while the harmonic scalpel is used to complete hemostasis following the lymph node harvesting.
The subcarinal region is visibly emptied of its contents following the dissection and removal of lymph nodes.
A surgical drain is placed in the thoracic cavity before reinflating the lung, followed by a chest x-ray showing the postoperative condition on the following day.
The discharge was on day two.
These are some technical details of the device.

To conclude, the harmonic can be safely adopted to reduce the cost of VATS lobectomy through reduction of stapler application, reduction of other instruments, offering safe blunt and sharp resection on vascular bronchial structures and finally a potentially more extensive and safer lymphadenectomy.
Thank you for your attention and thank you to all the thoracic surgery team at Manchester for providing this material.
